protocols.notes_updates: Scorpion stings may cause life-threatening envenomation | Species identification guide: scorpions with thick tails and slender pincers are more venomous than those with thin tails and large pincers | Scorpions are mainly nocturnal and active during summer months | Apart from local pain and occasional mild local inflammation, most scorpion stings are relatively harmless | In South Africa, only two Parabuthus species associated with severe envenomation: Parabuthus granulatus and Parabuthus transvaalicus | Severity usually evident within 15-60 minutes, but in adults systemic symptoms may be delayed for 8 hours | Children may present with unique restlessness marked by crying and thrashing - indication of severe systemic envenomation | Bulbar paralysis accompanied by respiratory failure is more frequent in children and primary cause of death | Differential diagnoses without history of sting: neurotoxic spider bites, snake bites, cholinesterase inhibitor toxicity, drug overdose, Guillain-Barre syndrome, tetanus | Scorpion antivenom should be given to all patients with symptoms and signs of severe envenomation | Standard antivenom dose: 5-10mL IV for both children and adults, diluted in small volume normal saline, given over 15 minutes | May take 2-6 hours to reach maximum effect | Additional dose of 5mL may be given after 6 hours if response inadequate | Patients kept under observation for 6-12 hours after antivenom administration | Pain management: if simple analgesia ineffective, infiltrate sting site with local anaesthetic | Opioids are relatively ineffective and increase risk of respiratory depression | Generalised muscle cramps: IV calcium gluconate 10% (Adults 10mL, children 0.5mL/kg) temporarily alleviates for 20-30 minutes | Support cardiorespiratory function as needed | Do NOT give antihistamines and steroids routinely | Tetanus toxoid if required | Poisons Information Helpline: 0861 555 777

Identification: 5 species of Latrodectus in southern Africa, 3 important: L. geometricus (brown button, brown to grey, up to 4 size variants with hourglass marking); L. indistinctus (black button, black with orange-red strip); L. rhodesiensis (East African button, neurotoxic variant found in northern Limpopo) | Webs: irregular messy tangle with funnel retreat, lower surface contains sticky globules of glue | Venom: potent neurotoxic venom. L. geometricus & L. indistinctus cause similar clinical features. L. rhodesiensis (rare) may cause a predominately neurotoxic illness similar to West African Lactrodectism | FANGS: chelicerae only bite weakly, venom injected through small puncture wound through hollow bristles on prosoma | Bites: most inflicted when spider is trapped against skin. Males not medically important (shorter fangs, less venom). Adult females more dangerous | Clinical features: Latrodectism - (1) General: local burning pain at bite site usually mild, becomes generalised abdominal/back/thigh cramps after 30-60 minutes, (2) Autonomic: regionalised sweating often the hallmark, may be regional or generalised, profuse sweating at bite site, (3) Neurological: headache, anxiety, restlessness, paraesthesia, hypersalivation, ptosis, (4) CVS: less common - hypertension, tachycardia, cardiac arrhythmias, (5) GIT: nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain/cramps, may mimic acute abdomen | Investigations: CK often raised. ECG if cardiac symptoms | Treatment: (1) General: Reassurance. Clean wound. Admit for observation. (2) Pain control: NSAIDs, oral/IV opioids (morphine). Local LA infiltration if needed. IV calcium gluconate 10% 10mL in adults (0.5mL/kg in children) - repeat once after 30 min if needed. (3) Specific therapy: Antivenom indicated for moderate to severe envenomation - significant pain unresponsive to simple analgesia, local or generalised sweating, abdominal pain/cramps, hypertension/tachycardia, restlessness. Dose: 5-10mL IV over 15 min. Repeated after 6 hours if response inadequate. (4) Discharge criteria: asymptomatic 6 hours post-antivenom, adequate pain control, 6-12 hours if no antivenom | NB: Each antivenom vial neutralises 4mg venom. Redback antivenom in prefilled syringes available. L. indistinctus antivenom is F(ab)2 preparation

South African ticks: approximately 20 species, 3 can cause tick paralysis in livestock (Rhipicephalus, Hyalomma, Amblyomma) | Tick bite fever (Rickettsia africae): transmitted by cattle ticks (Amblyomma hebraeum). Most common tick-borne disease in southern Africa | Clinical features: incubation 5-7 days, eschar at bite site, fever, headache, malaise, myalgia, regional lymphadenopathy | Treatment: Doxycycline 100mg BD for 5-7 days (or 200mg OD). Chloramphenicol 500mg QID for 5-7 days if pregnant children. Self-limiting even without antibiotics
